summary: The interview focuses on designing a YouTube-like system. Ravi outlines functional requirements (upload, watch, search) and non-functional requirements (availability, scalability, reliability, performance, latency). He then estimates key metrics like daily active users, video views, uploads, and storage. The high-level design includes an API Gateway, load balancer, and dedicated services. In the deep dive, Ravi details the upload process using blob storage for videos and SQL for metadata, incorporating a transcoder service for multi-device compatibility. The watch service leverages CDNs for low latency and adaptive streaming for varying network conditions. For search, he proposes data extraction, a key-value store for keywords, fuzzy search for spelling, and lazy loading for results, concluding with a review of scalability aspects like replication and queues.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Hey everyone, welcome to another system design mock interview. Today we're doing a very typical question that lots of you have requested, and to do it, we have a current FAANG engineer, or MAANG engineer as lots of people seem to say now. He's with us now, Ravi, thank you so much for doing this.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Thank you so much, Tom. Pleasure. How are you doing?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: I'm really good, thanks. Ravi, before we get started, quickly just give people an introduction into who you are as an engineer and a bit about your experience.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure. So, I currently work as a senior software engineer, as you told, in a MAANG company. I have around 11 years of experience; it's been an 11-year journey. I primarily specialize in distributed systems, backend stuff, primarily Java, JavaScript, a lot of frameworks, MongoDB, and a lot of other databases, and also in cloud tech. Yeah, I've had a really great journey throughout these 11 years, and I'm very, very happy or thankful for this opportunity that you're giving me.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Great. Well, I think it's going to be a really interesting interview, so let's crack on with it. Ravi, the question I'd like to ask you today is, how would you design a system like YouTube?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So, basically, we have to design a YouTube kind of system, all right? Okay. As a user, when I think about a system like YouTube, I majorly use it to watch videos. I also search videos, but I do understand that there are people who are uploading the videos also. Like, I myself am not that much of a content creator yet, but then people also get the option to upload videos, like they create their own content or they can upload their own videos. So, if I get this correctly, primarily, I think first, upload video should be done because once a video is uploaded, then we can watch it. So, let's say upload video is one thing which we would like to have here. Then we can have watch video functionality, which will enable users to watch. I also have a search functionality; I can basically do a search of all the videos. And then, when I talk about particular videos as such, we can like a video, dislike a video—not dislike, but we can like, comment, and all those things. I'm just thinking, does this look good? What are your thoughts?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, that makes sense to me so far.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, all right. I think with likes and comments and dislikes, I think we can leave that out of scope. So, let's just pass that by for now.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, sounds good to me. So, probably, yeah, I'll remove that for now. Okay, so that's something which, okay, I have clarity from the functional point of view that these are the features we are looking at. Now, a YouTube kind of system is a global system. So, talking from a distributed systems point of view, I want the system to have high availability, like highly available, I would say, because yes, if a system like YouTube goes down, it definitely affects the user experience and all. And I think there are times, or probably there are videos, where a lot of users are interested, so we also need to account for the scalability where we do not affect the user performance if the number of requests increases. So, what I can think of primarily at this point is these are the requirements. Does that look good to you, Tom?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, that looks good to me. I think let's also add reliability because once a video is uploaded, I think users need to be very certain that their video is going to be intact and it will be available once it's uploaded. So, let's add in reliability and also let's think about performance and latency. I don't want users to experience any lag or latency while they're watching a video.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, that makes sense, actually. Yeah, thank you for bringing that up because definitely, as a user, I don't like if the video is buffering and, you know, that high latency part. Okay, all right. So, yeah, this looks good. So, what I will do is I will just name them functional requirements and non-functional requirements. All right, fine. So, I would keep referring to these requirements time and again during the part of my design so that I can confirm that I'm on the right track. Cool, let's get started with this.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: At this point, I would say that I'll slightly talk about the high-level design.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So, from a very high-level perspective, what I will say, well, I think we can also discuss the APIs at this point, but then, as I'm thinking, I would first slightly describe the high-level view, and then as I go deep into the system, I would specifically relate the APIs. So, I think that will be a much more sensible thing to do. So, let's move to the high-level part here. So, the high-level design. Let's say we have a user here, all right? And again, I'm just trying to depict a user, pardon the drawing, but yeah, that's a user. The request comes, probably it can be an upload, it can be a watch. I'm not segregating the request right now; we'll talk about that. But the request comes, there's a black box which receives the request. And then what I'm thinking is, we would have a certain kind of things, probably, which will take care of—in fact, I should draw a box out here also—that a certain thing will take care of the upload part specifically. Then a separate thing will take care of the, so let me name them. So, this will be the upload kind of thing, so this will take care of the upload functionality. Then there will be a certain thing which will take care of the watch functionality. And then there will be a certain thing which will take care of the search functionality, I would say. And again, I am not going into very details right now because this is a high-level thing right now. We obviously will need to talk about the databases and all those things, how the video is getting stored and all those things. But yes, I would say that this is a very much high-level design. And this part which receives is kind of a front-facing thing for us. It might be components like API Gateway, load balancer; we'll talk about that. But this is pretty much a very high-level stuff what I would like to talk about. So, cool, I think we are good from a high-level design perspective.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I believe we are good to move ahead, so I'll talk about the API endpoints right now.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, that sounds good.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, and yeah, once I talk about the API endpoints, so right now I'm going into the deep dive section also, so I'll in parallel also deep dive. So, first, let me finish off with the API, I think, first and foremost. Yeah, so I will say, okay, so from an API endpoint, first of all, I would talk about the uploading part here. So, let me talk about the upload section here. So, when I talk about the upload part, precisely, I would say that there is going to be an upload method which would definitely take some kind of user ID, user ID which uploads the videos, like we try to capture which user is uploading the video. Probably each video will have its own video ID, I would say. There will be a video title for the video that will also be there. And I would say, maybe there can be some other fields, like again, in the interest of time, I'm not writing the data types of these, but user ID probably will be a string, video ID will also be a string, and similarly for video title. And there can be several other things, maybe some location and, you know, tags or something if you want to associate. So, yeah, I think those all things can be there, and that will be the upload thing. Now, I would say, primarily, how the upload will take care of that. When users, yeah, so when users upload the video, it gets uploaded. So, let me draw this box here. So, again, I'm expanding on each of these sections. So, first of all, when the request comes, there will be, I'll go with an API Gateway which will take care of any authentication or authorization part, and I would combine this with a load balancer. Now, the placement is, I would say, placement is very subjective. Like, we can have the API Gateway first, the load balancer next, and vice versa also is fine. But primarily, API Gateway is to take care of the authentication and authorization, and load balancer will take care of the load distribution, like from the high availability perspective and all those things, doing regular health checks and all. So, this is how it will flow. Okay, and now this is the section where I'm coming to. So, we are talking particularly about the upload part here. Now, I'm into the deep dive section right now. Okay, so in the upload part, I would say that once we receive a request for uploading the video, we capture certain details from the user device, details like, you know, how much is the video size and all those things, and what is the user ID, like they'll be uploading from their channel. So, we capture the user ID and all those things, and we would store in some kind of storage. The video will be stored in some kind of a storage. Yeah, so there will be some kind of a storage, and that will primarily, again, on a very high level, specifically how upload will, I would say, work. Do you have any questions here, Tom?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yes, what kind of storage will you use for the metadata and also for the actual video? And I also wanted to ask, how would you handle multiple upload requests without affecting user experience?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Right, yeah. I think that's a good question. I think I was thinking on the same line. So, yes, there is a lot of metadata, as I see, which we are capturing. So, yeah, let me think about it. What I had in mind from the beginning when I saw the question was, since this involves video or media content and all, I think from the storage perspective, S3 makes sense, like to store the actual video. I would say like S3 kind of a storage, or I should say a blob storage. I mean, S3 is very specific to AWS, but I would say a blob storage makes sense. Now, that I will use for the videos or storing the actual videos. And for the metadata, I think I will go with something like a SQL database. I would say a SQL DB for the metadata. I think I will go with that. Okay, yeah.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: And why would you do that?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: All right, okay. Yeah, so why would I do that? See, when I said S3 and all, S3 blob storage makes sense for that because that, I mean, S3 or a blob storage stores the content or files as simple blobs. There's no hierarchy as such, and the retrieval or the storage part, like the efficiency of the storage and all. So, primarily what I'm trying to say is S3 stands out in these use cases, like a blob storage stands out for media content, static content kind of thing, and that's why I will use S3. It also additionally conforms to our requirement of reliability where we want to ensure that once the video is uploaded, it will be intact. So, S3 promises, or any blob storage for that matter, promises very high reliability. So, that's why I'll confirm with blob storage; I'll go with blob storage for actual video. And for the metadata, why I'm saying so is, yeah, I can actually go for SQL because the data, like the schema, is not going to change very frequently. I think we'll be interested in a particular set of metadata for each video. And a certain, I would say, if I talk about a bit of metadata around that, so we can have something like, probably, video ID, as I told earlier, then we can have something like a video title, and probably user ID will also be there, location will be there, and there can be a few other fields. Again, I'm not going into all the things here, but probably there will be metadata for each video. I would also have a separate table to capture the user information, so that would have the user ID, username kind of thing, and so primarily, just to answer your question again, I'll just complete this, like there'll be email and then there'll be probably address or something like that just for the legality purpose and all. So, just to confirm to your question, I would use S3, I think I already answered that. For the metadata, SQL because we have a fixed data schema, and I don't think this is going to change, so I'll go with SQL for this part here.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, yeah. One more question: how would you handle multiple device requirements? So, there's only a single format that a video is uploaded in, right? But the requesting device might not find that format suitable. So, how would you handle that?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Right, okay. Yeah, that's also a good question. I think, yeah. So, one thing is, I talked about API Gateway from the authentication and authorization perspective, so that's one thing. But API Gateway also takes care of, again, one part of the answer here, I would say, is that requests coming from different devices would obviously pass through an API Gateway which will take care of, I would say, forwarding them appropriately as per the device from where it is coming. So, that's one advantage of API Gateway. However, I think your question particularly talked about like there are multiple devices and they all have their own format. So, the format part, I think, is primarily important here, and that is where I would go with the concept of encoding. I would say that encoding or transcoding is something which is important here. And at this point, I think something which makes sense is to kind of shift these components to the right a bit. Okay, and what I will do is I will introduce a component called transcoder service. And this service, what it does is that originally when the upload request comes, primarily there will be this service which takes care or handles that request, I would say. And it would, first of all, the raw video, the raw original video, will be directly uploaded to the S3 storage so that we confirm about the reliability aspect that yes, your video is stored. So, S3 also will help, I would say, for parallelizing the upload so that will increase the upload speed. So, if there are multiple requests also, we may have some kind of a queue here which takes care of handling multiple requests so that, I would say, the user experience is intact. Now, once the original video is uploaded, I think the transcoder service will fetch the video from the original storage, and then it would start its process. And I think we can have another queue here because again, there can be multiple steps that can be involved as part of transcoding. And for each, taking care of each step, I think we can have a queue to account for multiple steps. And as each request is completed, accordingly, there will be other sets of servers or worker nodes, I should say, which will pick out those completed tasks. And then accordingly, I would say, we can have a separate blob storage which will store the transcoded video into different formats. And once the different formats video is available for us, then we can take care of, according to the watch request where it is coming from, we can pick which particular will be suitable and we can serve it from there. So, I think that is how I will take care of that.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, yeah, well answered. Feel free to carry on with your design.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: All right, thank you so much. So, let me see, this is primarily how I would go about the upload thing. And again, there can be a lot of things here, like, you know, I can talk about the concept of chunking where video is divided into multiple chunks because that will take care of user experience where each video is divided into separate chunks. And since we have different devices, so we can set offsets against each chunk, and users can watch the same video from the point they left. And this concept will be facilitated with chunking. And also, this transcoding will help with the compression part where we can optimize on the storage. So, I think there's a lot of advantage here. Now, that's the, I would say, that's the upload part here. Okay, right. So, let me talk about the watch service now. All right, so I'll draw a separate box here. Again, I am expanding on this initial high-level design and talking about each request separately. So, this was the upload thing. So, if I move a bit down here, and again, this part will remain common, like API Gateway, load balancer, and all those things. So, I just copy this, I would bring it down. Oh, I scrolled a lot. Okay, so this is the watch thing that I'm trying to account for. So, the request comes from the load balancer, and then as per the request, this will be a watch service. So, accordingly, the relevant API will be invoked for the watch service, I would say. And what I think, for the watch, there will be an API called something like 'watch video'. And this one, again, I forgot to mention one thing, is that the upload part, when the API is called, it returns a success or a failure kind of a thing to the user. So, if the video is uploaded successfully, it will return a 200 kind of a thing, or a failure in case there's any issue. Similarly, the watch API here. Okay, so it would take something like, again, probably the user ID who is requesting the video so that we can use it for later purpose, then obviously the video ID, the video that they're watching. I think this is where the chunking part comes into picture, that we will also have the offset, offset where the user is, you know, probably it will take care of the last offset where the user left that particular video, will take care of that. So, that's what this is here. And then we would have the video resolution, you know, resolution so that we can account for the different, for which format the user is requesting the video from. So, that's something. So, I think primarily this is what we will have as part of the watch thing. And what this will return is, it will return the actual chunks, actual video chunks which the user wants to watch or which will constitute the video. So, now how this will work, so let me talk a bit about that. So, when the user sends a request to watch a video, I would say we determine, like we capture certain information, the metadata from the user device, like the video resolution, the video type, what resolution is suited, what screen size is suited, and all those things. And then based on that, we can choose, we can, I would say, go about picking out the actual video. So, let me draw this. So, we would go reach out to this blob storage where our transcoded video is stored. So, this is the transcoded blob storage, I would say, transcoded one. And based on that, we can fetch the video from there, I would say. So, that is, yeah, like that is something which I can talk about from the watch perspective. But I go back to the requirements and I see that we have a requirement of the system being performant, and we also need low latency. So, I would also introduce a CDN here, a component called CDN. Let me drag this down. So, I would introduce a component called CDN, which is basically Content Delivery Network, and it takes care of, basically, again, it stands out in these media use cases where it helps in bringing content closer to the user. So, I would kind of use CDN to enhance the user experience and improve on that low latency part. And not all the videos will go to the CDN. Again, we will have some kind of a video controller or a controller service which would decide, like, based on the request when it comes. So, I would say it goes to the controller, it checks if it is for a popular video or not. And if it is for a popular video, I think, like we can check that, okay, if that popular video, we can check which is the nearest CDN for that. The controller will have all that information. It can be a simple, you know, kind of a key-value store kind of a thing which keeps track of, according to the location of the user. Again, we can have location also from where the request is coming. So, based on the location of the user, which is the nearest CDN from where we need to fetch the video or the chunks, and accordingly, we can kind of fetch it to the user. If the video is not present in the CDN, I think we can refer to the original storage, but then accordingly, we will also populate the video in the CDN, I would say, so that for the next request onwards, we can get it directly from the CDN. So, this is primarily the focus from the low latency and performance aspect of the requirement that we had. So, that would be, I would say, I think that's how the watch service will work. Do you have any questions here?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, now obviously there are some situations where users will have varying networking conditions. You know, some users will have strong internet connections, and some won't. So, how will you take care of the viewing experience with this in mind? Like, will the video start buffering?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Right, okay. Right. So, yeah, I think for different conditions, again, yeah. So, primarily, I think you're talking from a networking aspect where different users have a, I would say, different network performance. And obviously, if I push a constant video resolution to all the users, they might not be able to adapt to it, right? Because, let's say, if the network, like the internet, is supporting the speed is only like 200 kbps or 1 Mbps, and I'm passing in a 4K video, it would kind of ruin the user experience, as I see, right? Okay. So, for that, I would go about, I think I need to focus on the protocol here. So, I would go about using an adaptive streaming algorithm here. And there are several protocols like MPEG, I think, if I'm not wrong. Again, I'm not very sure on this, but I think there might be several specific protocols like MPEG-DASH or something like that which support adaptive streaming. And what I mean by adaptive streaming is that the current bandwidth of the user is monitored all the time during adaptive streaming. And as the bandwidth, according to what the bandwidth at the user end is supported, I would say that the adaptive streaming protocol will take care of what is the best resolution which will be suitable according to this varying network bandwidth of the user. So, I would go for implementing an adaptive bitrate algorithm to take care of this situation where we will determine what bandwidth is supported on the user end, and according to that, we can choose or probably deliver the best-suited resolution for the user's device. I think that is how I will go about handling that situation.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, that works. Yep, please carry on.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: All right, okay, sounds good. Okay, I think, just checking my design from, I would say, from the requirement perspective. So, yeah, I think overall it looks good. I will capture the offset. I've talked about the CDN also. And I would say CDN will take care of the low latency and all those things. And yeah, I think I'll also, probably we can have some kind of replication also here. Again, I just saw the scalability requirement. So, thinking of the scalability aspect, I think we can have replicas, read replicas particularly. So, probably what I mean to say is that while the original video is being served from this blob storage or the CDN, where we will definitely have, I would say, multiple CDNs here, obviously, to kind of serve the increased request. But what I mean to say is that our service, we can have replicated architecture here. So, when I say replicated architecture, it's like something like, it's not like only a single server will be handling all the requests; multiple servers will be there. So, that's one point. And another point is that once a video is written to a particular CDN, let's say, then it will be replicated across CDNs. So, what I mean to say is that while one CDN will definitely fetch the videos, but then we can have other CDNs. So, one CDN will primarily work as the leader where it will serve as the source of truth, and the other CDN instances will copy from this leader. And accordingly, we can direct our watch request to the other CDNs also so that would distribute the load evenly if it increases. And I think that will take care of the scalability aspect here. So, that is something which we can add here, I think. And finally, I think from the search perspective, what I will do is, again, I will just copy all of this. And when a search request comes, what I will do is, okay, here's what I will do for the search thing. So, I think when a video is uploaded, I will do some kind of data extraction. So, I will extract whenever a video is uploaded, as we saw originally in the flow section here, that we are capturing a lot of metadata, right? Although I mentioned only a few fields, but there can be several other fields. But then, I would say, as part of that metadata, so that is where our data extraction will come into picture. And what I will do is I will extract data from that metadata and I will store, I will use a JSON file. So, I think, not in this, this, this, this, definitely, I would say, although I'm talking about for the search thing here, but let me draw the upload thing here also because this part will actually happen in parallel when the upload is happening. So, when the upload is happening, we will also work on the metadata, and there will be an extraction here that will be happening. And we will extract several information out of that metadata and store that in a JSON file. That's what primarily I will do. Now, we will have something like title of the video, channel name, description of the video, content of the video, probably like the different tags which people associate with a video. Now, in each JSON file, what I will do, the keywords will be extracted from these documents. So, this JSON file will have this extracted information. And what I will do, I'll further have a key-value database, maybe something like a Redis caching, something like that, key-value store, or maybe a DynamoDB also, like that also is a good key-value store. So, I will extract the keywords from the JSON document where I have got the extracted data. I will pull out some keywords and I'll store them in a key-value store. So, what basically will be in a key-value store is that the key, it will hold all the keywords, it will hold all the keywords which are searched by the user. And those will serve as the key, and against the value for each key will be the video IDs which conform to that key. So, for example, let's say if someone searches for 'food', 'food' is a keyword, and if there is any cooking video, any kind of food blogger who has uploaded videos, all these videos will go in the value as a list. All these video IDs will go against the key called 'food', something like that. So, that's what the key-value store will store. Okay, and what I will do is that, again, we can also kind of do some ranking that based on the frequency of how many searches have happened for that particular keyword, we can rank the entries in the key-value store so that when the user searches for any particular thing, any particular keyword, the most relevant videos, the most relevant search results can be fetched for the user. So, that's the idea here. So, that's primarily how I would say the search will work. Does that sound good to you?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, what about spelling mistakes? Sometimes users misspell words. How do you handle that?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, so I think for spelling mistakes, again, that would be something which I will handle from an algorithmic point of view where I would probably write some logic or implement some kind of a fuzzy search algorithm. So, let me just write this down. I would implement some kind of a fuzzy search algorithm. And fuzzy search algorithm primarily actually kind of, again, I'm not very sure right now how exactly it works behind the scenes, but primarily it stands out in these use cases where, you know, if you remember, when I search something on Google and if I have misspelled it, then Google suggests 'Did you mean something like this?' So, that is the effect of a fuzzy search algorithm. So, it kind of detects intelligently that probably the user might have meant this, so it will autocorrect your search term. So, I would go with talking about a fuzzy search algorithm to handle such use cases. Yeah, so I think that's from the search aspect. We can also probably have some kind of a queue, you know, let's say when the search results are returned. So, again, based on the key-value store, it will have the video IDs, and the actual videos will obviously be stored in the blob storage. So, when the response is returned to the user, what I mean to say, what I will do is I can have some kind of a queue here in the middle. Why so? Because probably I can do some kind of lazy loading for the search results where the users, like I can show them initial first 10 results while all the other results are still being uploaded, and still being fetched, I would say. And while I show them the first 10 results, while the other results are still being fetched in the background, I think a queue makes perfect sense here where I will keep on pushing the results as they are fetched. And as the user kind of scrolls through the page, I will implement the lazy loading and kind of fetch those results from the queue and display to the user. So, I think a queue also kind of makes sense here to me. So, overall, I would say, I think this is the design I want to go ahead with. Do you have any comments? Do you want to just go over it, review it?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, if you're ready, I think we've just got a couple of minutes left, but if you're ready with your design, I'll have a look and review it.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure, let me just do a quick revision of this. Probably, yeah, I have. And again, one thing which I just wanted to highlight is that although I have not, you know, drawn repeated boxes, I am cognizant of the fact that we do need scalability. So, one aspect of scalability is definitely load balancing, but load balancing also helps with, I would say, availability, in fact, not very much on the scalability. But one aspect of scalability is using the queues, and other is using replication. So, although I might not have drawn this, there are, I would say, replicated instances of several components like this upload service, the servers which will be serving those requests, and then this blob storage will have, or CDN, I would say, CDN will have replication across regions to take care of the scalability aspect. So, I think, yeah, overall, this is what I will go ahead with. Yeah.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Great, okay, let me just have a look at it.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, Ravi, well done. I think this design covers a lot of aspects of the requirements that we discussed, including the availability and scalability. You've used a leader-follower architecture, replication, and queues to assist with those requirements. I think you do that successfully, so well done.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: All right, thank you so much. Hello, really hope you found that useful. If you did, you can like and subscribe, and why not come visit us at igotoffer.com? There you can find more videos, useful frameworks, and question guides, all completely free. And you can also book expert feedback one-on-one with our coaches from Google, Meta, Amazon, etc. Thank you, and good luck with your interview.

training_pairs.question: Ravi, before we get started, quickly just give people an introduction into who you are as an engineer and a bit about your experience.
training_pairs.answer: Sure. So, I currently work as a senior software engineer, as you told, in a MAANG company. I have around 11 years of experience; it's been an 11-year journey. I primarily specialize in distributed systems, backend stuff, primarily Java, JavaScript, a lot of frameworks, MongoDB, and a lot of other databases, and also in cloud tech. Yeah, I've had a really great journey throughout these 11 years, and I'm very, very happy or thankful for this opportunity that you're giving me.

training_pairs.question: Ravi, the question I'd like to ask you today is, how would you design a system like YouTube?
training_pairs.answer: So, basically, we have to design a YouTube kind of system, all right? Okay. As a user, when I think about a system like YouTube, I majorly use it to watch videos. I also search videos, but I do understand that there are people who are uploading the videos also. Like, I myself am not that much of a content creator yet, but then people also get the option to upload videos, like they create their own content or they can upload their own videos. So, if I get this correctly, primarily, I think first, upload video should be done because once a video is uploaded, then we can watch it. So, let's say upload video is one thing which we would like to have here. Then we can have watch video functionality, which will enable users to watch. I also have a search functionality; I can basically do a search of all the videos. And then, when I talk about particular videos as such, we can like a video, dislike a video—not dislike, but we can like, comment, and all those things. I'm just thinking, does this look good? What are your thoughts?
training_pairs.follow_up: Yeah, that makes sense to me so far.

training_pairs.question: Okay, all right. I think with likes and comments and dislikes, I think we can leave that out of scope. So, let's just pass that by for now.
training_pairs.answer: Okay, sounds good to me. So, probably, yeah, I'll remove that for now. Okay, so that's something which, okay, I have clarity from the functional point of view that these are the features we are looking at. Now, a YouTube kind of system is a global system. So, talking from a distributed systems point of view, I want the system to have high availability, like highly available, I would say, because yes, if a system like YouTube goes down, it definitely affects the user experience and all. And I think there are times, or probably there are videos, where a lot of users are interested, so we also need to account for the scalability where we do not affect the user performance if the number of requests increases. So, what I can think of primarily at this point is these are the requirements. Does that look good to you, Tom?
training_pairs.follow_up: Yeah, that looks good to me. I think let's also add reliability because once a video is uploaded, I think users need to be very certain that their video is going to be intact and it will be available once it's uploaded. So, let's add in reliability and also let's think about performance and latency. I don't want users to experience any lag or latency while they're watching a video.

training_pairs.question: Yeah, that looks good to me. I think let's also add reliability because once a video is uploaded, I think users need to be very certain that their video is going to be intact and it will be available once it's uploaded. So, let's add in reliability and also let's think about performance and latency. I don't want users to experience any lag or latency while they're watching a video.
training_pairs.answer: Okay, that makes sense, actually. Yeah, thank you for bringing that up because definitely, as a user, I don't like if the video is buffering and, you know, that high latency part. Okay, all right. So, yeah, this looks good. So, what I will do is I will just name them functional requirements and non-functional requirements. All right, fine. So, I would keep referring to these requirements time and again during the part of my design so that I can confirm that I'm on the right track. Cool, let's get started with this.

training_pairs.question: All right. So, the first thing that I will do is I would like to get an idea about a bit of numbers around the system. What do you think? Let me ask you this question: how many total users are there for the platform? What do you think?
training_pairs.answer: Let's say two billion total users, right?
training_pairs.follow_up: Okay, yeah, YouTube is quite a popular platform, so I would just note down that as total users as two billion. Okay, sounds good. So, that gives me a reference point to start from. Let's say there are two billion users. I would say the number which I'm more interested in is how many users are using it daily because that will help me get clarity on the storage side of things and on the network side of things. Okay, so the daily active users, I would assume somewhere around 400-500 million users. Okay, does that look good to you?

training_pairs.question: Yeah, I think 500 million daily active users sounds right for me.
training_pairs.answer: Okay, and on that note, I think we also need to think about how many videos an average user watches per day because that is a good metric to account for. So, I would say something like number of videos per day per user. I think on average, I would go for five, like per user watches five videos per day. Yeah.

training_pairs.question: Do you have any questions here, Tom?
training_pairs.answer: Yes, what kind of storage will you use for the metadata and also for the actual video? And I also wanted to ask, how would you handle multiple upload requests without affecting user experience?

training_pairs.question: And why would you do that?
training_pairs.answer: All right, okay. Yeah, so why would I do that? See, when I said S3 and all, S3 blob storage makes sense for that because that, I mean, S3 or a blob storage stores the content or files as simple blobs. There's no hierarchy as such, and the retrieval or the storage part, like the efficiency of the storage and all. So, primarily what I'm trying to say is S3 stands out in these use cases, like a blob storage stands out for media content, static content kind of thing, and that's why I will use S3. It also additionally conforms to our requirement of reliability where we want to ensure that once the video is uploaded, it will be intact. So, S3 promises, or any blob storage for that matter, promises very high reliability. So, that's why I'll confirm with blob storage; I'll go with blob storage for actual video. And for the metadata, why I'm saying so is, yeah, I can actually go for SQL because the data, like the schema, is not going to change very frequently. I think we'll be interested in a particular set of metadata for each video. And a certain, I would say, if I talk about a bit of metadata around that, so we can have something like, probably, video ID, as I told earlier, then we can have something like a video title, and probably user ID will also be there, location will be there, and there can be a few other fields. Again, I'm not going into all the things here, but probably there will be metadata for each video. I would also have a separate table to capture the user information, so that would have the user ID, username kind of thing, and so primarily, just to answer your question again, I'll just complete this, like there'll be email and then there'll be probably address or something like that just for the legality purpose and all. So, just to confirm to your question, I would use S3, I think I already answered that. For the metadata, SQL because we have a fixed data schema, and I don't think this is going to change, so I'll go with SQL for this part here.

training_pairs.question: Okay, yeah. One more question: how would you handle multiple device requirements? So, there's only a single format that a video is uploaded in, right? But the requesting device might not find that format suitable. So, how would you handle that?
training_pairs.answer: Right, okay. Yeah, that's also a good question. I think, yeah. So, one thing is, I talked about API Gateway from the authentication and authorization perspective, so that's one thing. But API Gateway also takes care of, again, one part of the answer here, I would say, is that requests coming from different devices would obviously pass through an API Gateway which will take care of, I would say, forwarding them appropriately as per the device from where it is coming. So, that's one advantage of API Gateway. However, I think your question particularly talked about like there are multiple devices and they all have their own format. So, the format part, I think, is primarily important here, and that is where I would go with the concept of encoding. I would say that encoding or transcoding is something which is important here. And at this point, I think something which makes sense is to kind of shift these components to the right a bit. Okay, and what I will do is I will introduce a component called transcoder service. And this service, what it does is that originally when the upload request comes, primarily there will be this service which takes care or handles that request, I would say. And it would, first of all, the raw video, the raw original video, will be directly uploaded to the S3 storage so that we confirm about the reliability aspect that yes, your video is stored. So, S3 also will help, I would say, for parallelizing the upload so that will increase the upload speed. So, if there are multiple requests also, we may have some kind of a queue here which takes care of handling multiple requests so that, I would say, the user experience is intact. Now, once the original video is uploaded, I think the transcoder service will fetch the video from the original storage, and then it would start its process. And I think we can have another queue here because again, there can be multiple steps that can be involved as part of transcoding. And for each, taking care of each step, I think we can have a queue to account for multiple steps. And as each request is completed, accordingly, there will be other sets of servers or worker nodes, I should say, which will pick out those completed tasks. And then accordingly, I would say, we can have a separate blob storage which will store the transcoded video into different formats. And once the different formats video is available for us, then we can take care of, according to the watch request where it is coming from, we can pick which particular will be suitable and we can serve it from there. So, I think that is how I will take care of that.
training_pairs.follow_up: Okay, yeah, well answered. Feel free to carry on with your design.

training_pairs.question: Yeah, now obviously there are some situations where users will have varying networking conditions. You know, some users will have strong internet connections, and some won't. So, how will you take care of the viewing experience with this in mind? Like, will the video start buffering?
training_pairs.answer: Right, okay. Right. So, yeah, I think for different conditions, again, yeah. So, primarily, I think you're talking from a networking aspect where different users have a, I would say, different network performance. And obviously, if I push a constant video resolution to all the users, they might not be able to adapt to it, right? Because, let's say, if the network, like the internet, is supporting the speed is only like 200 kbps or 1 Mbps, and I'm passing in a 4K video, it would kind of ruin the user experience, as I see, right? Okay. So, for that, I would go about, I think I need to focus on the protocol here. So, I would go about using an adaptive streaming algorithm here. And there are several protocols like MPEG, I think, if I'm not wrong. Again, I'm not very sure on this, but I think there might be several specific protocols like MPEG-DASH or something like that which support adaptive streaming. And what I mean by adaptive streaming is that the current bandwidth of the user is monitored all the time during adaptive streaming. And as the bandwidth, according to what the bandwidth at the user end is supported, I would say that the adaptive streaming protocol will take care of what is the best resolution which will be suitable according to this varying network bandwidth of the user. So, I would go for implementing an adaptive bitrate algorithm to take care of this situation where we will determine what bandwidth is supported on the user end, and according to that, we can choose or probably deliver the best-suited resolution for the user's device. I think that is how I will go about handling that situation.
training_pairs.follow_up: Okay, that works. Yep, please carry on.

training_pairs.question: Does that sound good to you?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, what about spelling mistakes? Sometimes users misspell words. How do you handle that?

training_pairs.question: Yeah, what about spelling mistakes? Sometimes users misspell words. How do you handle that?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, so I think for spelling mistakes, again, that would be something which I will handle from an algorithmic point of view where I would probably write some logic or implement some kind of a fuzzy search algorithm. So, let me just write this down. I would implement some kind of a fuzzy search algorithm. And fuzzy search algorithm primarily actually kind of, again, I'm not very sure right now how exactly it works behind the scenes, but primarily it stands out in these use cases where, you know, if you remember, when I search something on Google and if I have misspelled it, then Google suggests 'Did you mean something like this?' So, that is the effect of a fuzzy search algorithm. So, it kind of detects intelligently that probably the user might have meant this, so it will autocorrect your search term. So, I would go with talking about a fuzzy search algorithm to handle such use cases. Yeah, so I think that's from the search aspect. We can also probably have some kind of a queue, you know, let's say when the search results are returned. So, again, based on the key-value store, it will have the video IDs, and the actual videos will obviously be stored in the blob storage. So, when the response is returned to the user, what I mean to say, what I will do is I can have some kind of a queue here in the middle. Why so? Because probably I can do some kind of lazy loading for the search results where the users, like I can show them initial first 10 results while all the other results are still being uploaded, and still being fetched, I would say. And while I show them the first 10 results, while the other results are still being fetched in the background, I think a queue makes perfect sense here where I will keep on pushing the results as they are fetched. And as the user kind of scrolls through the page, I will implement the lazy loading and kind of fetch those results from the queue and display to the user. So, I think a queue also kind of makes sense here to me. So, overall, I would say, I think this is the design I want to go ahead with. Do you have any comments? Do you want to just go over it, review it?
training_pairs.follow_up: Yeah, if you're ready, I think we've just got a couple of minutes left, but if you're ready with your design, I'll have a look and review it.
